description: Curated list of commonly consumed Indian packaged foods with nutritional concerns (high sugar, fats, sodium, refined carbs, additives) and better alternatives. For use in NutriPlan AI agents to give smarter, context-aware suggestions.

poor_choices.why_problematic: Classic glucose biscuit — extremely high in refined flour and sugar. Very low nutritional value. Often consumed in large quantities daily in India.
Marketed as healthy but still high in sugar and fat. Many variants use palm oil.
Popular tea biscuit but mostly refined carbs with little nutrition.
High calorie, sugar, and fat. Popular with kids.

better_alternatives.brand: Slurrp Farm / Lo! Foods / Eat Better Co
better_alternatives.why_better: Made with millets, often lower sugar or jaggery, higher fiber and nutrients. Better choice for everyday snacking.

better_alternatives.product: Whole Wheat / Oats Biscuits (low sugar variants)
better_alternatives.why_better: Choose ones with <5g sugar per serving and whole grains. Still consume in moderation.
Good source of calcium and fiber from ragi.

poor_choices.product: Haldiram's All in One / Bhujia / Sev
poor_choices.why_problematic: Extremely popular but very high in sodium (often 700-900mg per 100g) and fried in palm oil. Major contributor to daily salt intake.
High in salt, fat, and ultra-processed. Designed to be hyper-palatable.
Often fried in reused oil and loaded with salt.

better_alternatives.why_better: Much lower in sodium and fat when not heavily flavored. Good volume snacking option.

better_alternatives.brand: Slurrp Farm, Eat Better Co, Taali
better_alternatives.why_better: Baked instead of fried, made from millets. Significantly better nutritional profile.
Traditional, high protein, satisfying snack when not over-salted.
Better than fried namkeen for regular consumption.

poor_choices.why_problematic: Extremely high sodium (often 1000+ mg per pack). Very popular with children and students. Major source of ultra-processed food in Indian diets.
Similar issues to Maggi — high salt and low nutrition.

better_alternatives.product: Whole Wheat / Millet Noodles or Pasta
better_alternatives.why_better: Choose whole grain versions when available. Still limit frequency due to sodium in seasoning.
Best option — use minimal seasoning and load with vegetables.

poor_choices.why_problematic: Major source of added sugar. Linked to obesity, diabetes risk.
Often have as much sugar as soft drinks. Lack the fiber of whole fruit.
Common in Indian households, especially with kids. High sugar with almost no nutrition.

better_alternatives.why_better: Traditional, hydrating, and much lower in sugar.

poor_choices.product: Many Corn Flakes / Rice Flakes with added sugar
poor_choices.why_problematic: Many 'healthy' cereals in India are still high in sugar.

better_alternatives.why_better: Plain oats are excellent. Avoid pre-sweetened flavored versions.
Traditional and more nutritious than many packaged cereals.

general_guidelines_for_agents.when_user_has_many_poor_choices: Gently suggest swapping 1-2 items with better alternatives from the same category. Do not shame the user.
general_guidelines_for_agents.allergy_note: Always cross-check with user's declared allergies before suggesting any product.
general_guidelines_for_agents.frequency_advice: Even 'better' packaged options should be consumed in moderation. Prioritize home-cooked whole foods.
general_guidelines_for_agents.sodium_focus: Many Indian packaged savory items are extremely high in sodium. Highlight this when relevant for users with blood pressure goals.
